title: Script P2: Contig Assembly
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"753bj","text":"This protocol provides a method for assembly of metagenomic data using the Ray assembly toolkit and the subsequent analysis of contig statistics. Based on the publication: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a8acd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"946ia","text":"Hannigan, Geoffrey D., et al. \"The Human Skin Double-Stranded DNA Virome: Topographical and Temporal Diversity, Genetic Enrichment, and Dynamic Associations with the Host Microbiome.\" mBio 6.5 (2015): e01578-15.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":184,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"4rltm","text":"Required Software:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9aofg","text":"Ray-2.3.1","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8p6el","text":"bowtie2-2.1.0","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"btdh8","text":"Relevant Files","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1cunu","text":"Output:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5i4ul","text":"Virome_Sequence_Counts","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7li99","text":"Whole_Microbiome_Sequence_Counts","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9pd6","text":" Perl Scripts: calculate_abundance_from_sam.pl","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2qvvm","text":"R Scripts: R1 and R2","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":11,"length":2},{"key":1,"offset":18,"length":2}],"data":[]},{"key":"b48bp","text":"Python Scripts: get_trimmed_pairs.py","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"5BC0F609053149A18C988D666B8AAF68","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/Script-R1-Virome-Contig-and-Sequencing-Statistics-eh5bb86?step=undefined"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"AA92B6E5B19C49D48C0E9F7FC284FEB9","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/Script-R2-Whole-Metagenome-Contig-and-Sequencing-S-eh6bb9e"}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"mckm","text":"Perl scripts and other supplementary information available at: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5gs61","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e065j","text":"https://figshare.com/articles/The_Human_Skin_dsDNA_Virome_Topographical_and_Temporal_Diversity_Genetic_Enrichment_and_Dynamic_Associations_with_the_Host_Microbiome/1281248","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":171}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"987780C088F6483FA0C6954EEB615170","url":"https://figshare.com/articles/The_Human_Skin_dsDNA_Virome_Topographical_and_Temporal_Diversity_Genetic_Enrichment_and_Dynamic_Associations_with_the_Host_Microbiome/1281248"}}}}
steps: Contigs were assembled using the program Ray. Make directory for input files, separating based on forward and reverse reads. mkdir ./Ray/R1_for_raymkdir ./Ray/R2_for_ray mkdir ./Ray/R1_for_ray mkdir ./Ray/R2_for_ray

Copy over the pre-processed fastq files to respective directories. cp ./clean_phix_fastq/*R1* ./Ray/R1_for_raycp ./clean_phix_fastq/*R2* ./Ray/R2_for_ray cp ./clean_phix_fastq/*R1* ./Ray/R1_for_ray cp ./clean_phix_fastq/*R2* ./Ray/R2_for_ray

Then we used a custom script from the Bushman lab to get sequence pairs. Basically this means it went through the corresponding R1 and R2 fastq files for each sample and only kept sequences in each file that has a mate. mkdir ./Ray/R1_for_ray_pairsmkdir ./Ray/R2_for_ray_pairs Make output directories. mkdir ./Ray/R1_for_ray_pairs mkdir ./Ray/R2_for_ray_pairs Make output directories.

Search through all of the fastq files. https://figshare.com/articles/The_Human_Skin_dsDNA_Virome_Topographical_and_Temporal_Diversity_Genetic_Enrichment_and_Dynamic_Associations_with_the_Host_Microbiome/1281248 The python script get_trimmed_pairs.py is used in this step and is available in the supplementary information. for i in $(ls ./Ray/R1_for_ray); dofor j in $(ls ./Ray/R2_for_ray); do # Check to see if the sample ID's matchfor x in ${i::9}; dofor y in ${j::9}; do # If fastq files have the same sample ID, trim pairsif [ for i in $(ls ./Ray/R1_for_ray); do for j in $(ls ./Ray/R2_for_ray); do # Check to see if the sample ID's matchfor x in ${i::9}; dofor y in ${j::9}; do # If fastq files have the same sample ID, trim pairsif [ "$x" == "$y" ]; thenpython get_trimmed_pairs.py -f ./Ray/R1_for_ray/$i -s ./Ray/R2_for_ray/$j -o ./Ray/R1_for_ray_pairs/${i} -t ./Ray/R2_for_ray_pairs/${j}fidonedone done done

We first performed assembly for each individual sample. Generate ouput directory. It is important to note that the directory for Ray output should not be created before running Ray. This will halt the program and return an error. mkdir ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat

Write function to run Ray. Ray Assembly Toolkit 2.3.1 Unix https://github.com/sebhtml/Ray-Releases/ Jacques Corbeil runRayAcrossSamples () {FILE1=${1}_R1.faFILE2=${1}_R2.faecho $FILE1echo $FILE2mpiexec -n 9 Ray-2.3.1/Ray -minimum-contig-length 500 -p ./Ray/R1_for_ray_fasta/${FILE1} ./Ray/R2_for_ray_fasta/${FILE2} -o ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat/${1}}export -f runRayAcrossSamples runRayAcrossSamples () {FILE1=${1}_R1.faFILE2=${1}_R2.faecho $FILE1echo $FILE2mpiexec -n 9 Ray-2.3.1/Ray -minimum-contig-length 500 -p ./Ray/R1_for_ray_fasta/${FILE1} ./Ray/R2_for_ray_fasta/${FILE2} -o ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat/${1} } export -f runRayAcrossSamples
Run function. ls ./Ray/R1_for_ray_fasta | sed 's/\_R1\.fa//g' | xargs -I {} --max-procs=40 sh -c 'runRayAcrossSamples {}'

Rename output files so they contain the Sample ID. When they come out of the assembler, they are Contigs.fa- we rename them so they are MG100*_Contigs.fa. ls ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat | xargs -I {} mv ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat/{}/Contigs.fasta ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat/{}/{}_Contigs.fa

Additionally, each Contigs.fa has names and contigs in order. We want to add the Sample ID to the end of each contig ID to ensure they are all unique. for file in $(ls ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat); do#Remove block format in contig fasta file | Next three part of same thing | Replace the spaces in the contig names with underscores | Add sample ID to the end of each namesed -r 's/\s/_/g' ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat/${file}/${file}_Contigs.fa | sed 's/^\([A,T,G,C,n]*\)$/\1\@/g' | sed ':a;N;$!ba;s/\@\n\([A,C,G,T,n]\)/\1/g' | sed 's/\@//g' | sed '/\>/s/ /_/g' | sed /s/$/\_$file/" > ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat/${file}/${file}_Contigs_with_format.fa done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="F7FF34DA73C44EF29BDF56637D041DF8" > for file in $(ls ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat); do#Remove block format in contig fasta file | Next three part of same thing | Replace the spaces in the contig names with underscores | Add sample ID to the end of each namesed -r 's/\s/_/g' ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat/${file}/${file}_Contigs.fa | sed 's/^\([A,T,G,C,n]*\)$/\1\@/g' | sed ':a;N;$!ba;s/\@\n\([A,C,G,T,n]\)/\1/g' | sed 's/\@//g' | sed '/\>/s/ /_/g' | sed "/>/s/$/\_$file/" > ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_cat/${file}/${file}_Contigs_with_format.fa done

We also performed assembly for all of the samples concatenated together. Concatenate all fasta files together. cat ./Ray/R1_for_ray_fasta/* > ./Ray/cat_R1_pairs_for_ray.facat ./Ray/R2_for_ray_fasta/* > ./Ray/cat_R2_pairs_for_ray.fa cat ./Ray/R1_for_ray_fasta/* > ./Ray/cat_R1_pairs_for_ray.fa cat ./Ray/R2_for_ray_fasta/* > ./Ray/cat_R2_pairs_for_ray.fa
Run Ray assembler. mpiexec -n 25 Ray-2.3.1/Ray -minimum-contig-length 500 -p ./Ray/cat_R1_pairs_for_ray.fa ./Ray/cat_R2_pairs_for_ray.fa -o ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_total_cat_pairs
The contig output files from Ray are in block fasta format so we need to convert this to standard fasta format.
Rename the contigs.

Once we have assembled contigs, we want to determine the distribution information for these contigs. First, we calculate the total number of contigs and length of the contigs. mkdir ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_total_cat_pairs_contig_stats
Generate table with the sequence length of each contig. awk 'NR % 2 {printf $0
Remove extraneous characters from the table.

Additionally, we want to map our quality trimmed, decontaminated sequences against our contigs to determine the coverage of our contigs. mkdir ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_total_cat_pairs_contig_coverage_bowtie2

Build bowtie reference from the assembled contigs. Bowtie 2 2.1.0 Unix https://github.com/BenLangmead/bowtie2 Langmead B, Salzberg S. bowtie2-build -f ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_total_cat_pairs/Contigs_no_block_with_names.fasta ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_total_cat_pairs_contig_coverage_bowtie2/contig_bowtie2_build

Align samples to assembled contigs. bowtie2 -x ./Ray/ray_contigs_from_total_cat_pairs_contig_coverage_bowtie2/contig_bowtie2_build -f ./Ray/cat_R1_pairs_for_ray.fa -S ./Ray/cat_R1_pairs_for_ray_bowtie2.sam -p 32 -L 25 -N 1

Get abundance data from the bowtie output. https://figshare.com/articles/The_Human_Skin_dsDNA_Virome_Topographical_and_Temporal_Diversity_Genetic_Enrichment_and_Dynamic_Associations_with_the_Host_Microbiome/1281248 The perl script calculate_abundance_from_same.pl is used in this step and is available in the the supplementary information. perl calculate_abundance_from_sam.pl ./Ray/cat_R1_pairs_for_ray_bowtie2.sam ./Ray/cat_R1_pairs_for_ray_bowtie2_hit_counts.txt
Merge the contig length and bowtie hit values into a single tab-delimited file. awk 'FNR==NR { a[$1]=$2; next } $1 in a { print $1
Subsequent analysis of these results were performed in R.
creator: Geoffrey D Hannigan
